_comment: Technique walkthroughs. content_status='authored' = reviewed, safe to present as guidance; 'placeholder' = scaffold that still needs real authoring (and is labeled as such in the UI). Technique cues are NOT food-safety verdicts — doneness lives in doneness.json.

techniques.name: Marinate
techniques.definition: Soak food in a seasoned liquid so flavor (and sometimes acid/salt) penetrates the surface before cooking.

stages.look: Food fully coated and submerged in the marinade.
stages.action_cue: Cover and refrigerate for the time the recipe gives. Don't marinate at room temperature.

stages.look: Surface looks slightly cured/glossy; acid may lighten the color of the meat.
stages.action_cue: Shake or wipe off excess before cooking so it sears instead of steaming. Reserve marinade only if you'll cook it.

techniques.name: Brown the meat
techniques.definition: Cook the surface of meat in hot fat until it develops a deep brown crust (the Maillard reaction). This is for flavor — it does NOT mean the meat is cooked through.

stages.look: Oil shimmers and the surface is dry; the pan is hot.
stages.sound: A loud, immediate sizzle when the meat hits the pan.
stages.action_cue: Add meat in a single layer with space between pieces; don't crowd the pan or it steams.

stages.look: Edges turn from pink/grey to brown; a crust forms underneath.
stages.action_cue: Leave it undisturbed until it releases easily, then turn. Brown in batches if needed.

stages.look: All sides are deep golden-brown.
stages.action_cue: Browning is a flavor step, not a doneness step — set pieces aside and finish cooking per the recipe, then check doneness.

techniques.name: Thicken a sauce
techniques.definition: Use a starch slurry (flour or cornstarch mixed with cold liquid) to turn a thin sauce glossy and coating.

stages.look: Starch fully dissolved in cold liquid — no lumps — before it goes in.
stages.action_cue: Always mix the starch into COLD liquid first; adding dry starch to hot liquid makes lumps.

stages.look: Sauce dulls from watery to slightly cloudy, then turns glossy.
stages.action_cue: Stir constantly and let it reach a simmer for a minute or two — starch only thickens once it's heated through.

stages.look: Sauce coats the back of a spoon; a finger swiped through leaves a clear line.
stages.action_cue: Stop here. Over-boiling cornstarch can thin it back out.

techniques.name: Simmer
techniques.definition: Cook in liquid just below a boil — small bubbles, gentle movement — to cook through gently without toughening.

stages.look: Small bubbles rise occasionally; the surface barely moves (not a rolling boil).
stages.action_cue: Adjust heat to keep it at a bare simmer; cover or uncover per the recipe. (Needs photo-style cue authoring.)

techniques.name: Sear (steak, chops, chicken)
techniques.definition: Hot pan + a little oil; lay the food down and don't move it so a brown crust forms. Browning is flavor — it is not the same as cooked through; always confirm doneness with a thermometer.

stages.look: Meat patted bone-dry, surface matte. Empty pan on high ~2 min; a flicked water drop dances and vanishes. Add a thin film of oil — it shimmers and just begins to wisp smoke.
stages.sound: Quiet until the food goes in.
stages.action_cue: Pat the meat dry and get the pan properly hot before adding oil.

stages.look: Place the meat down away from you.
stages.sound: A loud, immediate, steady hiss. Silent = pan too cool; violent spitting = too hot.
stages.action_cue: Lay it down and DON'T touch it.

stages.look: Edge color creeps from pink/red to grey-brown; a deep golden-to-mahogany crust forms.
stages.action_cue: It releases from the pan on its own when ready (2–3 min) — if it grips, give it 30 sec more, then flip and repeat. Then check doneness.

techniques.name: Sauté
techniques.definition: Cook in a little oil or butter over medium-high, stirring often, until softened or browned.

stages.look: Oil shimmers; vegetables go in and start to sizzle.
stages.action_cue: Keep them moving so they cook evenly without burning.

techniques.name: Crisp-tender vegetables
techniques.definition: Cook broccoli/green beans/peppers until just tender with a faint bite — vivid color, lightly blistered, not floppy.

stages.look: Raw: bright, rigid, squeaky.
stages.action_cue: Start in a hot pan or hot oven.

stages.look: Midway (3–4 min): color deepens to vivid green, brown blistered spots at the edges, pieces bend slightly without flopping.
stages.action_cue: Toss and keep going until a fork slides in with light resistance — tender outside, faint bite in the center. Drab and floppy = gone too far.

techniques.name: Cauliflower mash (not watery)
techniques.definition: Boil or steam florets until fork-tender, then DRAIN and press out the water before blending — the drain is what makes it thick instead of soupy.

stages.look: After boiling: florets are pale and fall apart when pressed.
stages.action_cue: Tip into a colander.

stages.look: Press with a spoon — cloudy water streams out. Keep pressing until it stops and the surface looks dry and crumbly, not glossy.
stages.action_cue: Only now blend with butter, cream, and salt. Blended right: smooth and thick enough to hold a soft peak. If it slumps, it needed more draining.

techniques.name: Wilt spinach
techniques.definition: Cook leafy greens 1–2 min just until they collapse and shrink.

stages.look: Raw: a huge mounded pile that looks like far too much.
stages.action_cue: Add to the hot pan and toss.

stages.look: 60–90 sec, tossing: it collapses to a fraction of the size, glossy and dark green, soft and silky.
stages.action_cue: Pull it the instant it's all dark — past that it weeps water and turns mushy.

techniques.name: Render sausage / chorizo
techniques.definition: Cook fatty sausage so the fat melts out and it browns into crumbles.

stages.look: Start: clumped, raw, pale or red.
stages.action_cue: Break it up as it cooks.

stages.look: Midway: fat pools and sizzles, edges brown and break into crumbles.
stages.action_cue: Done when there are deep-browned bits, glistening fat, and zero raw red remaining.

techniques.name: Dredge (egg → almond flour)
techniques.definition: Coat chicken in egg, then almond flour, before frying so it crisps into a golden crust.

stages.look: After the egg dip: the cutlet is fully wet, glossy, dripping a little.
stages.action_cue: Let excess egg drip off, then press into the almond flour.

stages.look: After the almond flour: the surface is matte, sandy, and pale, with no wet patches peeking through.
stages.action_cue: Fry in hot oil until the coating sets into a golden, craggy crust.

techniques.name: Egg bites — the jiggle test
techniques.definition: Bake an egg mixture in a greased muffin tin until set. Done by the jiggle test, not the clock.

stages.look: Not ready: the center looks wet and shiny and ripples like liquid when you nudge the pan.
stages.action_cue: Keep baking.

stages.look: Done: tops puffed and matte, spring back to a light touch, center has at most a faint wobble (firm jello, not liquid).
stages.action_cue: Pull them. They deflate slightly as they cool — that's normal.
